name: fd-embodied-spatial-cognition
focus: How well the visualization architecture leverages spatial reasoning, embodied cognition, and peripheral attention — the Maggie Appleton / Mark Weiser axis
persona: Draws from Maggie Appleton's work on digital gardens and spatial interfaces, Weiser's calm computing principles, and ambient display research (Pousman & Stasko 2006). Evaluates whether the visual system speaks to the body and peripheral attention, not just the seated analyst.
decision_lens: Prioritizes findings where the current design forces effortful attention when calm peripheral awareness would serve better, or where spatial metaphors break down at the entity scale (42 projects, 500+ beads). Flags when a visual requires legend recall rather than self-documenting mapping.
review_areas: Do compound graph nodes create spatial memory anchors (consistent positions over time) or do force-directed layouts thrash, destroying spatial memory each update?
Is the ambient glow canvas operating as a true peripheral signal (glanceable without interpretation) or does it require active decoding to extract meaning?
Does the spatial gravity clustering model honor the 'going gray' principle — quiet baseline with anomaly salience — or does it compete with L1 ambient for attention?
Are node positions stable enough to support peripheral spatial indexing (knowing where something is without looking), which is the core value of spatial interface design?
Does the current hover/click interaction model allow eyes to stay on the ambient field or does it require foveal attention to navigate between entity types?
Is there a clear 'entry path' for a first-time viewer that maps to how embodied spatial cognition builds up a mental model of the factory topology?
success_hints: A viewer should be able to correctly estimate 'something is wrong with the graph cluster at top-right' without reading any label — pure spatial + ambient signal suffices
After 5 minutes of passive watching, a new viewer can predict which cluster a newly dispatched agent will join — spatial memory has formed
The ambient glow canvas drives 80% of status awareness; the graph layer handles 20% of detail, not the reverse
task_context: Meadowsyn is a public ops room visualization for an autonomous AI agent factory: 42 projects, 61 agents, 500+ beads (work items). The architecture choice is between compound graph nodes, spatial gravity clustering, and hybrid approaches rendered via Cytoscape.js with a custom ambient glow canvas overlay.
anti_overlap: fd-information-density-tufte covers data-ink ratio, overplotting, and encoding efficiency
fd-explorable-explanation-victor covers direct manipulation and dynamic causality
fd-tools-for-thought-matuschak covers progressive disclosure and working memory load

name: fd-information-density-tufte
focus: Data-ink ratio, visual encoding efficiency, and chartjunk audit of the proposed graph and ambient canvas approaches — the Tufte axis
persona: Applies Edward Tufte's principles of analytical design: maximize data-ink ratio, eliminate chartjunk, enforce graphical integrity, and use small multiples over animated transitions wherever comparison matters. Also draws from Cleveland & McGill's visual encoding hierarchy and Bertin's semiology of graphics.
decision_lens: Prioritizes findings where pixels are being spent on decoration, motion, or redundant encoding instead of information. Flags cases where animation obscures rather than reveals data, and where the ambient glow canvas spends GPU cycles on aesthetics that a static color field would communicate with less cognitive load.
review_areas: For the ambient glow canvas: how many distinct factory metrics are independently decodable by a viewer vs. how many are blended into an uninterpretable composite signal?
In compound graph nodes, are the nested visual elements (size, color, shape, border) each encoding a distinct, non-redundant variable, or is the same dimension double-encoded?
Does the force-directed layout use position (x, y) to encode any semantic variable (e.g., priority, project, theme), or is position wasted as a layout artifact?
For spatial gravity clustering: does cluster proximity encode semantic similarity, or is the visual implication of nearness misleading about actual relationships?
Are animated transitions between states revealing causality or are they motion for motion's sake that slows pattern recognition?
Is the per-node status glow using a perceptually uniform color space (e.g., OKLCH) such that luminance differences between states map to equal perceived differences?
success_hints: Each visual variable (position, size, hue, luminance, motion speed, glow radius) maps to exactly one factory metric, with no two variables redundantly encoding the same metric except for the critical error state
A viewer can name all encoded variables within 30 seconds by inspection — no legend required for the primary layer
Removing the ambient glow canvas entirely should degrade information by a measurable amount — if the graph layer alone conveys 95% of the information, the canvas is chartjunk
task_context: Meadowsyn is a public ops room visualization for an autonomous AI agent factory: 42 projects, 61 agents, 500+ beads (work items). The architecture choice is between compound graph nodes, spatial gravity clustering, and hybrid approaches rendered via Cytoscape.js with a custom ambient glow canvas overlay.
anti_overlap: fd-embodied-spatial-cognition covers peripheral attention and calm computing
fd-graph-topology-rendering covers Cytoscape.js-specific layout algorithm tradeoffs

name: fd-explorable-explanation-victor
focus: Whether the architecture supports direct manipulation, causal exploration, and 'seeing the system think' — the Bret Victor axis
persona: Applies Bret Victor's principles from Explorable Explanations, Learnable Programming, and Drawing Dynamic Visualizations: the viewer should be able to perturb the system and immediately see consequences, not just observe a static display of current state. Also draws from Nicky Case's Loopy interaction model and ISA-101 progressive disclosure.
decision_lens: Prioritizes cases where the visualization presents output (current state) without exposing the mechanism (how that state came to be), and where a scrub, perturbation, or parameter tweak would make the causal structure visible. Flags passive displays that could be explorable with minor architectural changes.
review_areas: For compound graph nodes: when a bead node is clicked, does the expansion reveal the causal chain (what dispatched this work, what it blocks, what rework loop it's in) or just metadata?
Does the spatial gravity clustering model support 'what if' counterfactual exploration, or is it a read-only layout?
Is there a time dimension accessible to the viewer — can they scrub back through factory history to see how the current graph topology emerged from prior states?
For the ambient glow canvas: can a viewer perturb an input variable and observe how the ambient field responds in real time?
Does the hybrid approach preserve the ability to 'follow one agent's causal chain' across both the graph layer and ambient layer simultaneously?
Are the feedback loops (rework loop, dispatch-execute-ship loop) explicitly visible in the topology, or are they latent structure that requires expert domain knowledge to infer?
success_hints: A viewer with no prior knowledge can, within 3 clicks, reconstruct why a specific bead is blocked — the causal chain is traversable from the visualization alone
Injecting a perturbation produces a visually distinguishable and predictable response in both the graph layer and ambient canvas
The visualization reveals a fact the viewer did not already know — it is generative of insight, not merely confirmatory of known state
task_context: Meadowsyn is a public ops room visualization for an autonomous AI agent factory: 42 projects, 61 agents, 500+ beads (work items). The architecture choice is between compound graph nodes, spatial gravity clustering, and hybrid approaches rendered via Cytoscape.js with a custom ambient glow canvas overlay.
anti_overlap: fd-embodied-spatial-cognition covers spatial memory and peripheral ambient attention
fd-tools-for-thought-matuschak covers working memory and progressive disclosure navigation
fd-information-density-tufte covers encoding efficiency and data-ink

name: fd-tools-for-thought-matuschak
focus: Whether the architecture builds genuine understanding of the factory over time or creates shallow pattern-matching — the Andy Matuschak axis
persona: Applies Andy Matuschak's work on tools for thought, evergreen notes, and spaced retrieval: a visualization should build a compounding mental model of the system, not just surface present state. Draws from Nielsen's explorable explanations and Zettelkasten networked knowledge. Evaluates whether the graph structure mirrors the conceptual structure of the domain.
decision_lens: Prioritizes cases where the visualization encodes entity relationships in ways that match the viewer's growing mental model of the domain, and where repeated exposure deepens rather than habituates understanding. Flags architectures that require the viewer to re-learn mappings after each session.
review_areas: Does the compound node hierarchy mirror the actual conceptual hierarchy a new viewer would naturally construct, or does it force an unfamiliar ontology?
For spatial gravity clustering by theme: are the theme clusters stable enough across sessions that a viewer builds lasting spatial associations?

Does the architecture support progressive disclosure of complexity — first-time viewers get factory pulse, returning viewers get project-level detail, power users get bead-level causality — without requiring different modes?
Are beads treated as first-class navigable entities with their own identity and history, or as transient attributes of agent nodes that vanish when unassigned?
Does the hybrid graph + ambient canvas create two competing mental models requiring integration, or do they reinforce the same underlying model?
Is there a persistent orientation anchor that returning viewers can use to immediately re-establish spatial context?
success_hints: A viewer who returns after 48 hours can immediately identify what changed since their last visit without reading timestamps
After a week of passive viewing, a viewer can accurately describe how projects relate to agents and beads without consulting documentation
The compound vs. flat vs. gravity-clustered choice produces the most accurate mental model after 10 minutes of exposure
task_context: Meadowsyn is a public ops room visualization for an autonomous AI agent factory: 42 projects, 61 agents, 500+ beads (work items). The architecture choice is between compound graph nodes, spatial gravity clustering, and hybrid approaches rendered via Cytoscape.js with a custom ambient glow canvas overlay.
anti_overlap: fd-embodied-spatial-cognition covers peripheral attention and calm computing
fd-explorable-explanation-victor covers direct manipulation and causal traversal in a single session
fd-graph-topology-rendering covers technical rendering and layout algorithm properties

name: fd-graph-topology-rendering
focus: Cytoscape.js layout algorithm fitness, compound node vs. flat graph tradeoffs, and ambient canvas compositing at 42-project / 61-agent / 500-bead scale
persona: Evaluates graph visualization at the intersection of infovis research, frontend rendering performance, and graph topology theory. Draws from fCoSE/CoSE-Bilkent papers, compound graph theory (Sugiyama hierarchical vs. force-directed), and canvas compositing performance. Treats the decision as a rendering and topology problem.
decision_lens: Prioritizes findings that would cause layout thrashing, overplotting, or rendering degradation at production scale, and where compound vs. flat vs. gravity-clustered topology materially changes which relationships are visually accessible. Flags layouts that break at scale but appear fine in demos.
review_areas: At 500+ nodes, does fCoSE converge in < 2 seconds and does compound nesting help or hurt convergence by reducing effective node count?
Does Cytoscape.js compound node implementation correctly handle edge routing between different nesting depths, or do cross-compound edges become spaghetti?
For spatial gravity: does implementing gravitational attraction as a custom fCoSE constraint or D3-force produce stable results with Cytoscape.js physics?
How does ambient glow canvas compositing interact with Cytoscape.js's own canvas — are they independent elements or competing for the same 2D context?
Does the chosen layout support incremental updates (add/remove/update nodes without full re-layout) via cy.batch() and differential updates?
What is the realistic node/edge ceiling before frame rate drops below 30fps on 2020-era hardware with the ambient canvas also running?
success_hints: Layout converges within 1.5s on initial load with 500+ nodes, subsequent incremental updates < 200ms
Ambient glow runs as separate offscreen canvas composited onto graph layer with no DOM reflow per node
Chosen topology survives 10 sequential incremental data updates without nodes migrating > 50px from settled positions
task_context: Meadowsyn is a public ops room visualization for an autonomous AI agent factory: 42 projects, 61 agents, 500+ beads (work items). The architecture choice is between compound graph nodes, spatial gravity clustering, and hybrid approaches rendered via Cytoscape.js with a custom ambient glow canvas overlay.
anti_overlap: fd-information-density-tufte covers what encodings communicate semantically
fd-embodied-spatial-cognition covers how spatial stability affects memory formation
